stimulus:
Text 1

What factors shape the long-term diversity of plant species in a meadow? Some theorists have argued that founding diversity is a major driver of how diverse a meadow community eventually becomes: differences in the diversity of grasslands occupying otherwise comparable sites, in this view, are strongly influenced by the number of plant species established in those sites at the very start.

Text 2

In 2015, a team of researchers led by ecologist Júlia Almeida established replicate experimental meadows on cleared farmland in southern Brazil. They seeded some plots with a richly diverse mix of native grasses and wildflowers and others with only a single grass species, then allowed the plots to develop without further intervention. Over the next five years, Almeida and colleagues regularly sampled plant species in the plots, finding—contrary to their expectations—that by the end of the study there was almost no difference in plant species diversity between the two types of plots.
stem: Based on the texts, how would Almeida and colleagues (Text 2) most likely describe the view of the theorists presented in Text 1?

answerOptions.content: It is largely correct, but it requires a minor refinement in light of the research team's results.
It is not compelling as a theory regardless of any experimental data collected by the research team.
It probably holds true only in conditions like those in the research team's study.
It may seem plausible, but it is not supported by the research team's findings.

rationale:
Choice D is the best answer. The view in Text 1 is that founding diversity strongly shapes long-term meadow diversity. Almeida and colleagues did not find this: by the end of their study there was almost no difference in diversity between plots that started out diverse and plots that started out with a single species. So they would describe the view as plausible-seeming but unsupported by their findings.

Choice A is incorrect because Almeida's findings directly contradict the view rather than refine it. Choice B is incorrect because the team's design was framed to test this hypothesis, and their results were "contrary to their expectations," meaning they once found the theory plausible. Choice C is incorrect because the findings directly undermine the view in conditions like those of the study.
